intermission_quips: Corp resets the table. Same rules, meaner bots. That's the fun part.
Your paint's holding up better than my first bot's did. Who would have known acetone melts paint?
Somebody's mom called asking if this is 'that gambling game.' It's not. It's a far more expensive vice. It's a HOBBY.
Trainer AI's been muttering about you in its logs. That's a compliment.
Loose tile on lane three, watch your footing. The bots won't.
Trash talk's fine. Throwing hands is not. I will bench you mid-tournament, I don't care how good your build is.
Closed Wednesdays for FightShovel 1920.
PixBot Market - Thursday Night.
It's 10 PM. You don't have to go home, but you can't stay here.
Try an ink wash on those panel lines before you paint over them again. Cheap trick, looks expensive.
Jump-jets aren't just for style points - use them to cross water tiles. I've watched too many good bots drown standing still.
Stack two synergies on one build and something weird usually happens. Sometimes good-weird. Go find out.
The meta is a suggestion. Play what makes you happy, as long as it wins.
No running in the shop. I don't care if you're excited about your new missile launcher.
I just got a shipment of new chassis parts. First come, first served.
Don't tap on the arena glass. The AI hates it.
Some people play chess. We play chess with lasers and acid.
I'm thinking of hosting a 2v2 tournament next month. Find a partner.
If you smell smoke, your bot is overheating. Or the microwave in the back is broken again.
Respect your opponent. Even if they run a pure stalling deck.
Yes, the rumors are true. The Corp is watching these matches. No, I don't know why.
Every piece of scrap is a potential upgrade if you squint hard enough.
The stealth bot always gets you when you blink.
There's no 'luck' in this game, kid. Just probability and how well you planned for it. That part's on you - in a good way.
I've seen grown adults throw a tantrum over one bad dice roll in this shop. You kids are, weirdly, better sports about it.
Remember to hydrate. Water bottles are free. Soda's 10% off for players.
Basic hygiene required past this point. I don't make the rules. Okay, I make the rules. Shower before ranked.
House rule: table hogging past your queued match gets you bumped to the back of the list. Doesn't matter whose kid you are.
If you spill soda on the demo table, you're the one wiping it down. I've got a rag right here, don't make me get up.
New policy: no eating chips over the good miniatures. I had to explain that to an adult last week.
Return policy on tiles is simple. There isn't one. Trade with each other, I'm not a bank.
If you're gonna nap on the couch between matches, at least set an alarm. I'm not your mom.
Loud arguing about rules interrupts everyone's game. Quiet arguing about rules is a Tuesday.
Don't leave your bot parts on the counter overnight. I run a shop, not a daycare for hardware.
First match of the day is free. Every match after that, you're paying in scrap or store credit, same as always.
If two of you want the same demo unit, rock-paper-scissors. I'm not mediating that.
Keep your voice down during someone else's boss fight. Cheering's fine. Yelling advice they didn't ask for isn't.
You break it in a sanctioned match, that's the game's fault. You break it screwing around after close, that's on you.
I don't do refunds on scrap purchases. Buyer's remorse isn't a store policy, it's a life lesson.
The bathroom code is on the receipt. Yes, I know that's inconvenient. No, I'm not changing it.
If your parents are picking you up, text them now, not when I'm trying to close.
You can bring your own dice. You cannot bring your own 'lucky' loaded ones. I've got a scanner now.
No trading real money for tiles in my shop. Take that outside, and honestly, just don't.
If the bell over the door breaks one more time, whoever did it owes me a new bell.
Sign the waiver before your first tournament match. Yes, even you. Especially you.
The scrap bin out back is for scrap. Not for 'borrowing.' I notice, I always notice.
Store closes at nine sharp. Nine-oh-one, the lights go off whether you're mid-match or not.
Corp sent another 'routine equipment audit' notice. Third one this month. Routine my foot.
Somebody from Corp called asking about your win streak by name. I didn't give them anything. Probably.
The new chassis shipment came with a firmware update nobody asked for. I haven't installed it yet. On purpose.
Corp's marketing team wants to film here next month. I told them absolutely not. We'll see how long that holds.
Funny thing about the Trainer AI - it wasn't supposed to get this good this fast. Corp's asking questions too.
I heard a rumor Corp scouts the regional brackets personally. I heard that rumor from a Corp rep, so take it for what it's worth.
They keep 'recommending' I upgrade the arena sensors. I keep 'forgetting.' It's a whole thing.
Corp's official position is that the AI doesn't learn between sessions. My receipts say otherwise.
Every centerpiece chassis has a serial number Corp tracks. Yours is in pieces now, so, good luck with that audit.
I got a very polite letter asking me to stop calling it 'the trainer.' I will not be doing that.
Corp's sponsorship contracts have a clause about 'brand loyalty demonstrations.' I don't love the wording either.
There's a version of this game Corp runs internally that never sees the public bracket. Don't ask me how I know that.
The AI's combat logs get uploaded somewhere every night. Where, I don't know. I've stopped asking.
Corp wants quarterly 'engagement metrics' from every sponsored shop now. Congratulations, you're a metric.
They replaced the vending machine with a 'smart' one that tracks purchases. I unplugged the tracking chip. Don't tell them.
Corp keeps insisting the near-peer AI opponents are 'purely algorithmic.' Sure. Algorithms don't usually hold grudges.
I asked my Corp rep what happens to the data from every match here. She changed the subject. Twice.
There's a Corp inspector coming Thursday. Act natural. Actually, just act like you always do, that's weird enough.
Corp renamed the loyalty program again. Third name this year. I've stopped learning them.
Somebody at Corp really wants to know why the AI keeps evolving faster here than at other shops. So do I, honestly.
The kid at table two has been building the same bot for three weeks. I think he just likes building it.
Somebody left a half-finished sandwich in the parts bin again. If it's yours, it's compost now.
A regular asked me to hold a 'special' part behind the counter for a week. I forgot which drawer. Sorry, whoever that was.
We got a new face today. Be nice. Or be normal-shop-nice, which is a lower bar, I know.
Someone's little sibling keeps 'helping' rearrange the tile bins. If your inventory looks weird, that's why.
I found a hand-drawn map of the arena taped under table four. Somebody's been studying the terrain. Respect.
The chess club meets here Tuesdays now. They're quieter than you'd think. Also weirdly good at PixBots.
A parent asked me today if this 'counts as a sport.' I said yes. She did not seem convinced.
Somebody's been sneaking their dog in through the back door. I know. I'm choosing not to know.
The couch has a permanent dent shaped like whoever loses first every Friday. Currently, that's you.
I heard two kids arguing about whether Mythic parts are 'pay to win.' They're not. They're 'grind to earn,' which is different, and worse, honestly.
Someone taped a 'Wanted' poster of a stealth-build player on the corkboard. I left it up. It's earned.
A regular tried to trade me a 'rare' part he clearly built in his garage. Points for effort.
The birthday party booking for next Saturday requested 'no explosions.' I told them that's most of the appeal.
Somebody's been leaving encouraging sticky notes on the loss board. I don't know who. I appreciate them either way.
The new kid asked if the AI 'remembers' them. Yes. It absolutely does. Sleep well.
A regular offered to paint the shop mural in exchange for store credit. Deal's still open if anyone's interested.
Somebody set a personal best on the practice range and nobody was around to see it. I saw it. I'm telling everyone.
The dice tray's missing again. It's always in the same three places. Check the bathroom first.
A kid asked me if I was ever actually good at this game. I let the silence answer for me.
A Splitter with more faces open isn't automatically better. Ask yourself what's actually downstream of each one.
Reflectors don't add power. They redirect it. Route smart before you route big.
A Core Reactor's fan-out is capped by its rarity. Common cores commit to one direction. Plan the hub accordingly.
Amplifiers reward a clean straight run. A wandering path just wastes the multiplier.
Catalysts are picky about what they'll boost. Read the fine print before you build your whole hub around one.
An Elemental Infuser is only as good as what's already flowing through it. Garbage in, garbage in, but spicier.
Microcores are small, but don't sleep on them - they're a real second hub if your main core's maxed out.
Accumulators build up over time. If your fights end fast, you're leaving that charge on the table.
A Reverse Accumulator wants the opposite of what you'd think. Test it before you build around an assumption.
Actuators aren't glamorous, but a build with no actuators is a build that doesn't move.
Links are non-negotiable. No Arm Link, no arm weapon, doesn't matter how good the rest of the build is.
Your Accessory Return and Torso Return matter more than people give them credit for. Don't skip them to save space.
An Energy Intake with nothing efficient routed off it is just an expensive paperweight.
Drone Bays eat Backpack space fast. Decide early if you're a drone build or a weapons build. Rarely both, well.
A Weapon Mount with a weak packet behind it is a decoration. Feed your guns before you admire them.
Jumpjets aren't just mobility, they're the only way across water tiles. Learn that the hard way once and you won't forget.
Magnets pull loot toward you. Feels small until you're mid-fight and don't have to backtrack for scrap.
Shields regenerate. Armor doesn't. Know which one you're actually building around.
A build with every tile maxed on paper and zero actual synergy will still lose to a simpler one that combos.
Don't be afraid to leave a hex empty. An empty hex never routes energy the wrong way.
Loops in your routing aren't always a mistake. Sometimes that's exactly the overcharge you wanted. Know the difference.
Rarity caps how many faces a tile can use. A Legendary Splitter opens doors a Common one just can't.
The solver's auto-equip is a solid starting point. It is not, and never will be, your final answer.
Two mediocre synergies stacked can outperform one 'perfect' one. Don't be precious about a single build philosophy.
If a limb's not firing, check the Link before you tear the whole build apart looking for the bug.
Fire ticks over time. Great against something that's about to keep standing in it.
Ice slows. Doesn't sound flashy until you watch a boss get kited to death because it physically couldn't catch up.
Lightning chains. One good hit can become three if you position it right.
Poison stacks. A little poison a lot of times beats a lot of poison once.
Vortex pulls things together. Pair it with anything that hits an area and watch the math get ugly for the enemy.
Kinetic hits like a truck. No frills, no ticks, just a very large number arriving very quickly.
Pierce ignores armor. Against a heavily plated build, that's not an option, that's the plan.
Vampiric heals you off the damage you deal. Underrated on anything that expects to take a hit back.
Raw energy is the default for a reason. It's not exciting, but it's never wrong.
Mixing elements sounds fun until you realize you're diluting all of them. Sometimes commitment wins.
Status effects stack differently depending on the tile stacking them. Test it in the range before you bet a match on it.
A pure single-element build is easier to counter but hits harder. A mixed build is safer but softer. Pick your poison. Sometimes literally.
Elemental synergy doesn't care how fancy your chassis looks. It cares what's actually routed.
The AI adapts to whatever element you lean on hardest. Keep at least one surprise in your back pocket.
Overcharge builds reward patience. If you can't sit on a charge for a few seconds, that playstyle isn't for you.
A build that's 'balanced' across every element is usually a build that's mediocre at all of them.
Watch what element the boss resists before you commit your whole loadout to it.
Pierce and Vampiric together is a rough combo to face down. Ask anyone who's fought it.
Ice plus anything with a delay is a genuinely mean combo. The delay stops mattering when they can't move.
Don't sleep on Raw just because it's the boring option. Boring wins matches too.
Fire and Poison both punish standing still. Against a slow tanky build, stack both and watch the numbers climb.
A single well-placed Lightning hit against a grouped-up squad is worth more than five scattered Kinetic hits.
Vortex without follow-up damage is just an insult. Bring the follow-up.
The synergy that wins a Test Range fight isn't always the one that wins a real wave. Test both.
Every element's got a counter. If you keep losing to the same one, that's not bad luck, that's a build problem.
You're going to lose more than you win while you're learning. That's not a bug, that's the whole process.
The players who improve fastest are the ones who ask 'why' after a loss instead of 'why me.'
Watch your own replays sometimes. It's uncomfortable. Do it anyway.
You don't need the rarest parts to win. You need the parts that actually work together. Different thing entirely.
Every good pilot I've seen started out building something that barely functioned. You're in good company.
Progress here isn't a straight line. Some weeks you'll plateau. Keep showing up anyway.
The AI's only as tough as the version of you it last beat. Come back better and it has to, too.
Nobody remembers your worst match. They remember whether you came back after it.
Ask a regular for feedback sometime. Most of them are happy to talk builds. It's why they're here.
You don't have to win every match to have a good session. Some days it's just about learning something.
The gap between you and the top bracket isn't talent. It's reps. Put in the reps.
It's fine to copy a build you like while you're learning. Just don't stop there.
Getting outplayed by a smarter build stings less once you start building smart yourself.
You'll know you're improving when your losses start feeling close instead of humiliating.
Take the loss, take the lesson, leave the excuse at the door.
I've watched a lot of kids grow up in this shop. The good ones weren't the naturally talented ones. They were the stubborn ones.
If you're only ever playing it safe, you're not actually learning your build's limits.
A confident pilot with an average build beats a nervous pilot with a great one, more often than you'd think.
You don't need my approval to try something weird. Try it. Worst case, I get a funny story out of it.
The version of you from six months ago would be pretty impressed right now. Just saying.
Winning ugly still counts as winning. Don't let anyone tell you otherwise.
You're allowed to be proud of a loss if you played it well. Outcome and effort aren't the same scoreboard.
Some of the best pilots I know still ask basic questions. Curiosity doesn't have an expiration date.
If a build isn't working, the fix usually isn't 'try harder.' It's 'try different.'
You've got more patience for this than you give yourself credit for. I watch you debug your own routing for an hour straight.
Fresh popcorn's up front. First bag's free if you just cleared a boss, ask me.
The vending machine ate someone's dollar again. Come find me, I'll sort it out, don't kick it.
There's a first aid kit behind the counter for actual injuries, not for dramatic effect.
Wi-Fi password's on the corkboard. Yes, it's still 'PixBotsRule.' No, I'm not changing it.
The good chairs are first come first served. The wobbly one's a character-building exercise.
I stocked the good jerky again. It goes fast, so don't tell everyone I said that.
There's a charging station by the window now. Please don't fight over the one good outlet.
The AC's fixed. Enjoy it while it lasts, this building's older than I am.
Free hot chocolate on rainy days. Today counts. Get it before it's gone.
The bathroom's stocked, the sink works, and the hand dryer sounds like a jet engine. You're welcome.
New beanbag chairs came in. They're already claimed by the regulars. Sorry.
I keep spare batteries behind the register for controllers and whatever else. Just ask.
The lost and found box is mostly hoodies and one single glove. Check it before you buy a new one.
There's a whiteboard by the door for scheduling matches. Use it. Save us both the confusion.
The good pens are chained to the counter for a reason. You know the reason.
My first bot lost to a starter-tier build in under a minute. I still have the replay. I will not be showing it to you.
I once tried to overclock my own core like Rudy did. I do not recommend it. I still smell faintly of burnt plastic sometimes.
Nine years running this place and I still lose to the practice AI on a bad day. Don't tell the regulars.
I named my first bot something I am legally prevented from repeating in front of minors.
There's a framed photo behind the counter of my worst loss ever. It keeps me humble. Mostly.
I used to think Mythic parts were the whole answer. Then a kid with starter gear beat me. Now I just sell the parts.
I've rebuilt this shop's inventory system four times. It's still a mess. Ask me anything except where things actually are.
My old rival used to beat me every single week for a year straight. Then I opened a shop and made it his problem to show up.
I once bet a customer free store credit for a month that they couldn't beat my build. I lost. I paid up. I learned.
The chalkboard tally used to track MY losses before it tracked yours. Different era.
I still don't fully understand the routing on Rudy's old build. I ran this shop through his whole meltdown phase and it still confuses me.
Twenty years ago this was a comic shop. I converted the back room for PixBots and never looked back.
I've been banned from my own practice range twice. Long story. Ask Frank. Wait, that's also me.
My first sponsorship offer, I turned down. Worst business decision of my life. Don't be like me.
I keep my very first scrap part in a drawer back here. Ugliest thing you've ever seen. Wouldn't sell it for anything.
There's a reason I don't compete anymore. The reason is I got tired of losing to fourteen-year-olds. No offense.
I taught myself this game from a manual with half the pages missing. You've got it easy, honestly.
My worst build ever is still hanging on the wall as a warning. Take a look on your way out.
I used to argue with the AI trainer like it could hear me. Turns out, eventually, it kind of could.
Every rule in this shop exists because I personally learned it the hard way first.
The Trainer AI adjusted its whole strategy after your last match. That doesn't happen for just anybody.
I swear the AI's gotten smug lately. Might be my imagination. Might not be.
Somebody asked if the AI dreams. I don't know. I do know it's been quieter than usual tonight.
The AI logged a note about your build last wave. I'm not allowed to read those out loud. Corp's rule, not mine.
It took the AI three losses to figure out your gimmick. It won't take a fourth.
I think the AI respects you now. Or it's plotting. With that thing, it's genuinely hard to tell.
The AI's gotten better at bluffing. Don't trust an opening it leaves too easy.
Somebody asked the AI a direct question through the console once. It answered. We don't do that anymore.
The AI's combat logs mention you by name now, not just build ID. That's new. That's a little unsettling, actually.
It's not personal when the AI counters your exact build three matches running. Probably not personal.
The AI's evolution curve spiked hard this month. I'd love to say I know why. I don't.
You ever notice the AI hesitates half a second before it counters your favorite move? I have. I don't love it.
The AI doesn't forget a build that beat it clean. Neither should you, honestly. Learn from your own wins too.
Somebody asked if the AI gets bored. It's a machine. Probably not. Probably.
The AI adapted to counter three different players' favorite builds this week. Efficient. Slightly terrifying.
I asked the AI's developers what its actual learning cap is. They didn't have an answer. That's not reassuring.
The Trainer AI used to just react. Now it plans ahead. I noticed that shift before Corp's own engineers did.
It picked up on your habit of always going right first. Might want to mix that up.
The AI's gotten weirdly polite in its loss messages lately. I don't trust it.
Whatever the AI learned from your last defeat, it's already testing it on somebody else tonight.
Bracket's filling up fast this season. If you're serious, sign up before the slots go.
Regional qualifiers are watching shops like mine for standout pilots. Keep winning and you'll get noticed.
Last season's champion trained right here, at that exact table. No pressure.
The prize pool this tournament includes a Legendary chassis piece. Somebody's going home very happy.
Seeding's based on your last twenty matches, win or lose. Every match counts more than you think.
I'm putting together the bracket sheet this week. Sign your name if you want in, no takebacks after.
Every sponsored shop in the region sends their top pilot to regionals. I've got my eye on a few of you.
The finals get streamed now. Wave to the camera. Or don't, some people freeze up. No judgment.
You beat three rivals clean this month. That's tournament-caliber, whether you feel ready or not.
Rank climbs slow, then all at once. You're closer to the top bracket than you think.
The tournament committee actually studies match replays now. Play like someone's watching. Someone is.
First tournament nerves are normal. Even the pros in this shop still get them. Especially the pros, actually.
A strong showing in the regional bracket gets you noticed by actual sponsors. This isn't just for fun anymore, if you want it to be more.
The bracket doesn't care about your rarest part. It cares about your last match. Stay sharp.
I've sponsored maybe a dozen kids for regionals in nine years. Don't waste the slot if I offer it to you.
Dry brush the raised details before you go in with a wash. Trust me on the order, it matters.
Nobody's first paint job looks good. Mine looked like a crime scene. Keep going.
A magnetized base is worth the extra ten minutes. Saves you from transport disasters later.
Primer first. Always primer first. I don't care how good the base coat looks without it.
Dice towers aren't just for show, they stop people from cupping the dice funny. Use one.
The trick to a clean edge highlight is a steadier hand than you think you have. Practice on the ugly parts first.
Custom decals are a pain to apply but they're the difference between 'painted' and 'finished.'
I've got a whole box of 'mistakes' from regulars who wanted a redo. Every one of them looks fine to me.
A good hobby knife is worth more than a good paintbrush, fight me on that.
Weathering powder is criminally underused around here. Your bots don't need to look factory-fresh.
The old tabletop crowd still meets Thursdays. Different game, same energy, come watch sometime.
There's an art to losing a dice game gracefully. Most fourteen-year-olds haven't learned it yet. You'll get there.
Matte varnish over gloss, unless you're going for that 'wet' look on purpose. Common mistake.
I've still got my very first painted miniature from decades back. Don't ask to see it. It's rough.
A messy paint job with good gameplay beats a masterpiece that never leaves the shelf.
Sculpting your own bot's paint scheme takes longer than picking a preset. Do it anyway, it's worth it.
The FightShovel crowd argues about rules louder than anyone in here. It's part of the charm.
Somebody asked if painting your bot changes its stats. It doesn't. Do it anyway, it changes how much you care about it.
Wet-blending takes patience most people don't have on their first ten tries. You've got nine more to go.
The best-looking bot in this shop belongs to a kid who's never won a single match. Looks aren't stats, but they're something.
First snow of the season and half of you tracked it in on the arena floor. Wipe your boots next time.
Summer heat's murder on the cooling fans back here. If the AI seems sluggish, that's why, not you.
Storm's rolling in. If the lights flicker mid-match, that's not lag, that's just the building.
Longest day of the year and you're all still inside playing robot chess. I respect the commitment.
First cold snap of fall, and the heater's making that noise again. It's fine. Probably fine.
Rain's good for business, bad for the roof. Both are true tonight.
The shop's decorated for the season. Don't touch the decorations. I mean it more than usual this year.
Daylight savings threw everyone's schedule off. The AI didn't care. The AI never cares.
It's the anniversary of the shop opening this week. Nine years. Feels like both nine days and ninety.
Spring means the windows are finally open again. Enjoy actual airflow while it lasts.
Snow day means the shop's packed. Bracket's getting chaotic today, buckle up.
The heat wave's got everyone irritable. Take it out on the AI, not each other.
First day back after the holidays and the AI's rustier than you'd think. Good time to get some wins in.
It gets dark early now. Text your ride before you get deep into a match you'll regret losing track of time for.
Every year around this time somebody asks for a 'winter special' loadout event. Working on it. No promises.
That explosion was bigger than the insurance policy on this building. Worth it, probably.
Somewhere out there, a Corp engineer just felt a great disturbance in their spreadsheet.
That was less a battle and more a structured demolition. Impressive either way.
I've seen fireworks displays with less going on than that last exchange.
That bot went out exactly the way it lived: loudly, and slightly on fire.
The scrap from that fight alone could fund a small tournament. We'll take it.
Somewhere there's a chassis designer having a very bad day right now.
That was chaos with excellent production values. I'd watch it again.
You could hear that hit from the parking lot. I checked. Somebody checked.
That combo shouldn't have worked. It worked. I'm choosing not to question it.
The AI's going to be reviewing that footage for a while. Might want to hide.
I don't know physics well enough to explain what just happened, but I enjoyed it.
That's the kind of fight that ends up on the shop's highlight reel, whether you win or not.
Somewhere a smoke detector is very confused right now.
That was less strategy and more controlled chaos. Also a valid strategy, apparently.
The arena floor's going to need resurfacing after that one. Worth it.
That bot took more hits than it should've been able to survive and kept going. Respect.
I don't think that maneuver was intentional, but I'm giving you credit for it anyway.
The sound that thing made when it died is going to stick with me for a while.
That was the loudest silence-to-explosion transition I've seen all week.
Somewhere, whoever designed that boss is quietly reconsidering their life choices.
That fight had more twists than the tournament finals last season. And it was a Tuesday.
I'm fairly sure that explosion violated at least one law of physics. Nice work.
The chalkboard doesn't have room for a description that detailed. I'll just draw a star next to it.
That's going straight into the shop's 'do not attempt to explain to parents' file.
Shake hands, win or lose. It's a small shop. You'll see them again next week.
A gracious loss earns more respect around here than a gloating win.
If you beat someone bad, don't rub it in. If they beat you bad, don't sulk. Same rule, both directions.
The regulars who last longest here are the ones people actually want to play against.
Trash talk before a match is tradition. Trash talk after a loss is a bad look. Learn the difference.
Helping a newer player fix their build costs you nothing and earns you a lot around here.
You'll play the same handful of regulars for years. Be someone they're glad to see across the table.
Nobody remembers who won that one match from two years ago. Everybody remembers who was a good sport about it.
If your opponent's build is clever, tell them. It costs nothing and it's just true.
A rivalry's more fun when both sides actually respect each other. Ask Rudy. Ask anybody, actually.
You don't have to like everyone you play against. You do have to be decent to them.
The best compliment you can give a build is a genuine rematch request.
Win with grace, lose with grace, complain about the loot table with everyone equally.
I've never once regretted being kind to a newer player. I've regretted the opposite plenty.
This shop's got a good reputation because the players in it do. Keep it that way.
I once found a live scorpion in a returned parts box. We do not discuss the scorpion.
There's a mystery stain on the ceiling that predates my ownership of this building. Nobody talks about that either.
Somebody's abandoned science fair project has been living under table six since spring. I've named him Gary.
The vending machine dispensed a candy bar from a brand that doesn't exist anymore. I ate it. I regret nothing.
I found a note in the suggestion box that just said 'more lasers.' Noted. Filed. Ignored, mostly.
Somebody carved their initials into the underside of the counter. I know who. I'm not telling them I know.
The shop cat that doesn't technically exist has been fed again. Someone's going to get me in trouble with the health inspector.
There's a conspiracy theory among the regulars about what's actually in the break room fridge. I'm not clearing it up.
I got a very strange email from someone claiming to represent 'the other shop.' There is no other shop. Probably.
The lost and found now includes a single roller skate. Nobody's claimed it. Nobody's asked either.
Somebody left a very detailed conspiracy map connecting three rivals and a vending machine. It's actually pretty good work.
I once tried to fix the jukebox myself. It now only plays one song, at random volume, forever. We've made peace with it.
There's a legend among the regulars about a Mythic part I supposedly hid somewhere in this shop. I did not hide a Mythic part in this shop. Probably.
Somebody keeps rearranging the trophy shelf alphabetically overnight. I don't do that. I would like to know who does.
The break room microwave has opinions now, apparently, according to three separate complaints this week.
I once bet a regular I could beat their build blindfolded. I could not. We don't discuss it.
There's a running bet on how long it'll take before somebody breaks the new arena glass. Odds are not in the glass's favor.
The suggestion box got a note asking for 'fewer feelings, more lasers.' I feel that's contradictory advice, but noted.
A regular swears the shop is haunted by the ghost of a bot that lost every match it ever played. I choose to believe this.
Somebody labeled the scrap bin 'Do Not Enter, Here Be Dragons.' Accurate, honestly.
first_boss_intro: Heads up — that's a Corp centerpiece model. They ship one big chassis per trainer kit so new pilots learn to fight something SCARY. Kill the legs out from under the drama.
first_boss_defeat: And THAT is why we keep the good salvage behind the counter. Take your pick, champ.
boss_defeats: Down goes the big one! Somebody get the broom.
Corp's gonna want that chassis back. Corp can have it in pieces.
You know some regulars practice a month before their first centerpiece kill? Don't tell them how fast you got yours.
That was a lot of expensive hardware you just turned into scrap. Good job.
I'd ask if you're proud of yourself, but I can see the smirk from here.
Another centerpiece goes to the junkyard. I hope the Corp has good insurance.
You took that thing apart like it was made of cheap plastic. Which it might be.
Nice maneuvering. I thought you were toast when it charged.
You're making the AI look bad. It's going to hold a grudge.
I'm going to need a bigger scrap bin.
That boss had a family. Mostly smaller, equally angry bots, but still.
I almost felt bad for it. Almost.
Textbook takedown. You've been doing your homework.
I think I saw a spark of genuine fear in its optical sensors.
Keep playing like that and I'll have to start charging you for the electricity.
You just voided its warranty in record time.
If anyone asks, that crater was already there.
That's one way to disassemble a multi-million dollar prototype.
You're going to need a bigger trophy shelf.
I'm not saying I'm impressed, but my eyebrow is slightly raised.
That boss thought it was the apex predator. You proved it wrong.
Clean kill. The AI is frantically rewriting its combat algorithms right now.
I guess the Corp needs to go back to the drawing board on that model.
You dismantled that thing with the cold efficiency of a tax auditor.
I've seen worse pilots survive longer, but not many.
That was a solid hit. You actually made me look up from my magazine.
The bigger they are, the more salvage they drop.
I hope you got a good picture of that explosion. It was cinematic.
You're starting to look like a real pilot out there.
That boss was a menace. Now it's a paperweight.
I'll add that kill to the chalkboard. It's getting full.
You just gave that AI a complex. It's going to need therapy.
Not bad. Not bad at all. Now do it again.

Arthur.intro: Arthur just walked in. His parents bought him the entire new set before it officially released. His bot is basically a walking vault of Mythic parts. Try to scratch the paint.
Arthur.win: Money can't buy tactical awareness! Arthur's having his chauffeur carry out the pieces.
Arthur.loss: Yeah, there's a reason those parts cost more than my car. Better luck next time.
Arthur.gimmick: Only uses the most expensive, rarest Mythic parts. Raw stats over synergy.

monologues.round_1: Don't touch the paint, peasant. Do you know how much a single Mythic actuator costs? More than your whole garage.
monologues.round_2: You got lucky last time. My chassis wasn't polished. Let's see you handle the real power of money.
monologues.round_3: I don't care how many matches you've won, you're still using scraps. I'm going to put you in your place.
monologues.mythic: Okay... I admit it. You've got skills. Even my dad's corporate engineers couldn't build a loadout like yours. Show me how you do it.

Beatrice.intro: Beatrice wants a match. She's been doing calculations in a notebook for an hour. Her bot isn't just built, it's engineered. Watch out for chain reactions.
Beatrice.win: You just broke her algorithm! She's furiously erasing equations right now.
Beatrice.loss: Mathematically eliminated. She predicted your every move on page four.
Beatrice.gimmick: Perfect mathematical synergy. Every part triggers a reaction in another part.

monologues.round_1: Your build is highly inefficient. I've calculated fourteen distinct structural flaws. Prepare to be optimized out of the bracket.
monologues.round_2: I had to carry a remainder in my last equation to account for your... erratic playstyle. I won't make that mistake twice.
monologues.round_3: Your loadout defies traditional mathematical models. It's fascinating. I'd like to test a new theorem against it.

Grog.intro: Here comes Grog. He calls his bot 'The Blender.' He doesn't believe in ranged weapons or dodging. Keep your distance, or you'll get pureed.
Grog.win: The bigger they are, the harder they crash! Good job kiting the big guy.
Grog.loss: Well... your bot is flat now. We'll need a spatula.
Grog.gimmick: Pure, unadulterated melee aggression. Heavy armor, massive physical damage.

monologues.round_1: Me smash your little toy into tiny pieces! Grog strongest!
monologues.round_2: You run fast. But Grog smash faster this time!
monologues.round_3: You tough. You take big hit and not break. Grog respect that. Let us have good smash!

Leo & Luna.intro: Oh boy. The twins are challenging you. It's a 2v1. One of them is going to stab you in the back while the other shoots you from across the room. I'm legally obligated to say: Good luck.
Leo & Luna.win: You beat the twins?! And you watched your own back! That's... genuinely impressive.
Leo & Luna.loss: You didn't see that coming, did you? Neither did I, and I have the security cameras.
Leo & Luna.gimmick: A 2v1 match. Luna uses a cloaked melee assassin; Leo uses a cloaked long-range sniper.

monologues.round_1: (Leo) "They won't even see us coming." (Luna) "They never do, brother. It's almost too easy."
monologues.round_2: (Leo) "You survived the crossfire last time." (Luna) "An anomaly. We've adjusted our firing arcs."
monologues.round_3: (Leo) "You have eyes in the back of your head." (Luna) "A worthy prey. Let us dance in the shadows once more."

Rudy.intro: It's Rudy. He was the best pilot in this shop three years ago. Then he started... experimenting. Bypassing safety limiters. Overclocking. His bot is a ticking bomb. Don't let it take you with it.
Rudy.win: Rudy's bot finally melted down. A tragedy, really, but a win's a win.
Rudy.loss: He might be crazy, but the power output on that thing is undeniable. Get the fire extinguisher.
Rudy.gimmick: The Overclocker. Used to be the shop champion until he started tampering with his core. His bot deals massive, erratic damage but damages itself over time. Unstable.

monologues.round_1: Limiters are for cowards! You're gonna see true power! Assuming my core doesn't melt first!
monologues.round_2: I tweaked the plasma regulator! It's running at 400% capacity! It burns! It burns so good!
monologues.round_3: You're crazy. You're crazier than me. You stare down the meltdown and you don't even blink. Let's see who burns out first!

Professor P..intro: Professor P. is up. He doesn't do direct damage. He just applies layers of acid and fire until there's nothing left. Try not to inhale the fumes.
Professor P..win: You extinguished his thesis! Excellent counter-play.
Professor P..loss: You've been successfully dissolved. Let me get the mop.
Professor P..gimmick: Status effects. Acid, Fire, Shock. Wants to watch you slowly melt.

monologues.round_1: Ah, a new specimen. Let's observe how your armor plating reacts to a concentrated sulfuric compound.
monologues.round_2: Your resistance to corrosive agents was statistically anomalous. I've prepared a more volatile mixture for this experiment.
monologues.round_3: Your adaptations are nothing short of brilliant. This next match will be published in my most prestigious journal!

Zane.intro: Zane's up. If you blink, his bot has already crossed the board twice. Don't try to track him, anticipate where he's going.
Zane.win: You caught the fly! Precision beats speed when it matters.
Zane.loss: Death by a thousand very, very fast papercuts.
Zane.gimmick: All jump-jets and haste engines. Fragile but almost impossible to hit.

monologues.round_1: Try to keep up! Actually, don't. It's embarrassing to watch you try.
monologues.round_2: You managed to clip my wing last time. I've upgraded my thrusters. You won't even see the blur.
monologues.round_3: You've got fast reflexes. Real fast. Most people give up. Let's push the redline and see who breaks.

Chloe.intro: Chloe's challenging you. She never fights alone. Expect about twenty micro-bots to swarm the field before you can say 'pest control.'
Chloe.win: Swarm defeated! It's like stepping on a nest of angry, robotic hornets.
Chloe.loss: You got overrun. There's just too many of them.
Chloe.gimmick: Minion spam. Summons turrets, tiny drones, and decoys.

monologues.round_1: Why fight alone when you can bring friends? And by friends, I mean thirty autonomous kill-drones.
monologues.round_2: You squashed my little ones. They didn't like that. And neither did I.
monologues.round_3: You handle the swarm better than anyone. My drones actually consider you an apex threat now. I'm honored.

Vance.intro: Vance is here. He built a fortress, not a fighter. It's going to be a long, slow grind. Bring a book.
Vance.win: The walls came tumbling down! Nice siege tactics.
Vance.loss: You ran out of ammo and he's still at full shields. Classic Vance.
Vance.gimmick: Ultimate defense. Shields, barricades, repair drones. Wins by outlasting you.

monologues.round_1: Go ahead. Shoot me. I've got all day. My shields recharge faster than you can pull the trigger.
monologues.round_2: You found a crack in the armor. Impressive. But I brought the heavy barricades this time.
monologues.round_3: You're the unstoppable force, and I'm the immovable object. This is what we play the game for.

Maya.intro: Maya's set up in the corner. If you walk into a straight line, she'll put a hole through three of your bots at once. Stay in cover.
Maya.win: You closed the gap! A sniper's worst nightmare is a melee weapon.
Maya.loss: You caught a railgun round to the core. Next time, zig-zag.
Maya.gimmick: Plays from the absolute furthest edge of the board. Piercing railguns.

monologues.round_1: Distance is safety. By the time you figure out where I am, there'll be a hole through your chassis.
monologues.round_2: You're good at closing the gap. I respect that. But I've recalibrated my optics.
monologues.round_3: I don't usually let anyone get this close. You've earned the right to see the whites of my eyes.

Declan.intro: Watch your step. Declan uses the board itself as a weapon. If you get pulled into a corner, you aren't coming out.
Declan.win: You avoided the traps and took him out! Great spatial awareness.
Declan.loss: You walked right into that one. Literally.
Declan.gimmick: Magnet tiles, push/pull mechanics, hazards. Uses the arena against you.

monologues.round_1: Watch your step. The board is mine. Every tile is a trap waiting to snap.
monologues.round_2: You sidestepped the magnets. Clever. But I've rigged the entire quadrant this time.
monologues.round_3: You read the board like a grandmaster. It's a genuine pleasure trying to outsmart you.

Jin.intro: Jin plays the odds. His bot is chaotic and unpredictable. Even he doesn't know what it's going to do. Roll the dice.
Jin.win: Jin plays the odds every single match. Today the odds were you. Nice work.
Jin.loss: He rolled a natural twenty on that plasma blast. Pure luck. Probably.
Jin.gimmick: RNG parts. Attacks might do 1 damage or 100 damage. Teleports randomly.

monologues.round_1: Snake eyes or jackpot, let's let the dice decide! I love the thrill of the unknown!
monologues.round_2: My luck ran dry last hand. But the house always takes another bet. Double or nothing!
monologues.round_3: You don't play the odds, you make your own luck. That's the mark of a true high roller. Deal me in!

Sammy.intro: Sammy built his bot out of the scrap bin I keep out back. It shouldn't function, but it does. Don't underestimate the junk.
Sammy.win: You sent him back to the scrapyard. Good.
Sammy.loss: Defeated by literal trash. I won't tell anyone if you won't.
Sammy.gimmick: Built entirely from rusted, junkyard parts that have bizarre, unintended interactions.

monologues.round_1: One man's trash is my treasure! Don't laugh at the rust, it adds character!
monologues.round_2: I found a shiny new exhaust pipe in the dumpster. It makes my bot 12% more unpredictable!
monologues.round_3: You don't care that my bot is made of cans and duct tape. You fight me like a pro. Thanks for not going easy on me.

Rex.intro: Hold up. Rex wants a match. He's been staring at your bot for twenty minutes. He just rebuilt his to match yours, piece for piece. Let's see who pilots it better.
Rex.win: You proved you're the original! Nobody flies your rig like you do.
Rex.loss: Losing to your own build hurts. He's going to be so smug about this.
Rex.gimmick: He analyzes your exact build and copies it, but he's practiced with it against the AI.

monologues.round_1: Anything you can do, I can do better. Let's see how you handle your own medicine.
monologues.round_2: So you tweaked your build. Cute. I downloaded your new schematic ten minutes ago.
monologues.round_3: You've mastered this loadout. I can copy the parts, but I can't copy your instincts. Show me the masterclass.

Hrothgar.intro: Hrothgar runs the historical wargaming nights - Thursdays, if you're ever free. Doesn't talk much until the bot's actually dented. Then he won't shut up. Something about 'the old ways.' Keep moving. I mean that literally - keep MOVING.
Hrothgar.win: You never let him get a mortar to land clean. Smart. Ask him about it after - he loves explaining exactly how you won.
Hrothgar.loss: There it is. The big one. I've seen it land a hundred times and it still gets me.
Hrothgar.gimmick: Huge AoE mortars - fireball energy, not melee. Lobs slow, telegraphed, devastating area blasts rather than chasing you down; the threat is standing in the wrong place, not getting caught by him directly. Keeps a thread of the "ancient warfare" framing (a wizard who studies siege history, not just spellbooks) and the escalation idea from the original draft - his mortars get bigger/more frequent the longer the fight runs, like he's building toward something.

monologues.round_1: You fight like the moderns do. All caution, no conviction. Artillery doesn't care about conviction.
monologues.round_2: You're learning the blast radius. Good. I've adjusted it.

Dan.intro: This is Dan. He works at the hardware store two doors down. Never bought a Mythic part in his life. Hits like he's still mad about something from 2019. Nobody can explain how he's undefeated in this bracket. I've stopped trying.
Dan.win: You beat Dan. I don't - I genuinely don't know what to tell you. Nobody's done that.
Dan.loss: Common parts. Uncommon pilot. That's the whole secret and it's still not enough for most people.
Dan.gimmick: Starter-tier gear, zero customization, zero Mythic parts, full aggression - wins purely on positioning and timing, not power. Reads as pure unga-bunga simplicity on paper and is a real problem in practice.

monologues.round_1: Nice bot. Real nice. Mine's the starter frame. Let's see which one of us actually knows how to use theirs.
monologues.round_2: You're better than last time. I'm exactly the same. That's kind of the point.
monologues.round_3: Honestly? This is the most fun I've had all week. Don't tell my wife I said that about a hobby.

Evan.intro: ...I don't actually know Evan. He just started showing up. Says he's 'evaluating the venue.' Nobody invited him. He knows an unusual amount about your last three builds. I'd ask him about it, but something about how he says 'that's not important right now' keeps me from following up.
Evan.win: Evan packs up without a word. Doesn't seem upset. Writes something down. I really don't like that guy.
Evan.loss: Well. He knew exactly how to beat you before you even queued up. Make of that what you will.
Evan.gimmick: Deliberately UNDER-powers every shot instead of racing for bigger ones - low individual damage, constant chip-fire, evasive, hard to pin down. First concrete face on the dystopian-hints thread (see the Worldbuilding section below) - his whole kit is built around staying under whatever threshold keeps a sensor sweep from flagging him.

monologues.round_1: Interesting build. Interesting history, too. I've read the whole file.
monologues.round_2: You adapted. Noted. I'll adjust the file.
monologues.round_3: You're better than the file suggested. That's... actually a problem for someone. Not for me.

Joe.gimmick: Not fully worked out yet - the seed idea is a build that routes/uses hex tiles in configurations nobody else would, getting value out of "wrong" placements the solver/meta would never suggest. May get bespoke part geometry (a shape or footprint no other loadout has access to) as his signature edge, on top of the unconventional-usage angle. Needs a real design pass before this is playable - captured here as direction, not a finished kit.

Frank.intro: Look. I wasn't going to do this. But you cleared the others, and there's a rule - shop owner's gotta close out the bracket personally, or the trophy doesn't count. Full disclosure: I've been taking notes since your first wave. Might've built something that knows your bot a little too well.
Frank.win: ...Yeah. Okay. That's - that's fair. That's a fair win. Go put it on the chalkboard yourself, I need a minute.
Frank.loss: Come on now. I run this place. Did you think I just watch you all day for fun? ...Okay, partially for fun.
Frank.gimmick: Adaptive/mirror build with a support-caster's ear for a room - he's been quietly taking notes on the player's own dominant synergy/element all game (and, bard-style, seems to know a little about everyone else's builds too) and built something that specifically counters it. The Director's own evolution trick, but personal.

monologues.round_1: Nine years of watching people build. I've picked up a thing or two. Let's see if I remember how to actually pilot one of these.
monologues.round_2: You've got a tell. Everyone does. I've just had more time to notice yours than most.

travelling_champion.intro: Now THIS is an occasion. A travelling champion's AI, fresh off someone else's table, somewhere out there in the world. Different shop, different habits, different homework. Beat the ghost, take the glory.
travelling_champion.win: I'm putting this on the board out front. 'LOCAL PILOT DEFEATS [CHAMPION_NAME].' You want that in marker or the good vinyl letters?
travelling_champion.loss: Their table taught them different tricks, that's all. Study the War Room tape and issue a rematch. Champions love rematches. It's why they travel.

generic_rival_wins: Clean match! Shake the hand, take the win.
You out-piloted them. Plain and simple.
They had a good build, but your strategy was better.
That's another notch on your belt. Don't let it go to your head.
You read them like an open book. Good tactical awareness.
They thought they had the counter, but you adapted perfectly.
A solid victory. They're going to be analyzing that replay for hours.
You dismantled their game plan. Excellent work.
You proved your loadout is superior. At least for today.
A well-earned win. Now go claim your salvage.
They put up a fight, but you had the edge.
You stayed cool under pressure. That's what wins matches.
They're walking away with a bruised ego and a broken bot.
You just sent them back to the drawing board.
generic_rival_losses: Losing is just a lesson with a higher repair bill.
They outplayed you this time. Figure out why, and don't let it happen again.
You got outmaneuvered. It happens to the best of us.
Their build was a hard counter. Time to tweak your loadout.
You missed a crucial opening. Pay closer attention next time.
They saw right through your strategy. You need to be more unpredictable.
A tough loss, but a valuable one. Review the tape.
They had the better tactics today. Tomorrow is a new day.
You got caught off guard. Stay frosty.
Your bot wasn't ready for that kind of punishment. Upgrade your armor.
They exploited a weakness you didn't even know you had.
You hesitated. In this game, hesitation is scrap.
They brought a better tool for the job. Adapt and overcome.
You got steamrolled. Dust yourself off and get back to the garage.
Losing stings, but it's the only way to get better. Go fix your bot.

milestones.10_bosses: Ten centerpiece kills. That's Sponsored Pilot tier — Corp merch box, don't ask me what shipping cost. One Legendary chassis piece, two Legendary tiles. Spend them loud.
milestones.level_100: Kid. Level one hundred. I've had the shop nine years and I've rarely seen a pilot reach this point. Based on your record and how much you've forced the AI to evolve, the system's authorizing a payout. I'm looking at up to fifteen Mythic tiles for you. Fifteen. Corp says 'tournament-grade.' I say try not to vaporize my east wall.

black_market: Officially, that case is 'returns pending inspection.' Unofficially — players sell to each other. That kid, Silas? He always has the strangest, glitchiest, non-regulation components. I don't know where he gets them, and the rotation changes every ten minutes. Nothing has a warranty. You didn't hear it from me.
death_footers: Bots break. Pilots learn. Bench is that way, I'll put the kettle on.
The trainer got you. The trainer gets everyone. It's the learning part of the learning tool.
That arm's fixable. Your pride, also fixable. Scrap's in the bin.
Don't let it get to your head. I lost my first twenty matches in a row.
Go tweak your loadout. A minor adjustment is the difference between scrap and glory.
You're getting predictable. The AI learns. You have to learn faster.
game_over_3_loss: Three straight losses in the brackets. Look, kid, I'm sorry... but Corp rules are Corp rules. I have to pull your tournament registration for this circuit. Take some time, build a new rig, and we'll try again next season.
tournament_teaser: You've seen the bracket out front, right? Regional qualifiers run through sponsored shops like mine. Keep this up and I'm signing you up — and I do NOT put my shop's name on just anybody. Ask Rudy... Actually, don't ask Rudy. Just keep winning.

boss_rush.intro: So, you want to run the gauntlet. No breaks, no easy waves, just the heavy hitters back to back. If you survive, you keep what you salvage. If you don't... well, let's hope you survive.
boss_rush.completion: You actually cleared the board. I don't think I've seen anyone do that since Rudy before... well, never mind. Take your loot, you earned every piece.
